Welcome to the MisArch project - A representable microservice reference architecture

See Architecture for the architectural design of the system, including the domain model, component diagram, and a specification for use cases and business processes. Components & Services for detailed documentation of every domain service or system components ADRs for the architectural decisions
The model shows all entities and relationships of the reference ecommerce shop. info Bounded contexts that are implemented within one service are highlighted with a purple frame.

When creating a relation between entities of different services, we need to check if the referenced related entities managed by a different service are valid to use. Most importantly, does the given ID actually refer to an entity.
import related entities into service by listening to dapr creation events created by the owning service send a request to the owning service to verify that the entity with a given ID is valid

Chosen option: "import related entities into service by listening to dapr creation events created by the owning service", because results in more loose coupling, as we don't have synchronous requests between services when creating the relations. Note that there might be exceptions in cases where this approach is not applicable.

Good, because more loosely coupled: no requests sent when creating the relation, instead async notifications via the event bus when entities are created Neutral, because potentially more/less events/requests: reduces communication overhead when related entity is on average used more than once, otherwise increases overhead Bad, because less enforced consistency: e.g. deletion events might be delayed, can cause temporary inconsistencies (as usual with loose coupling)
documents.s: import related entities into service by listening to dapr creation events created by the owning service

documents.t: Good, because results in stricter consistency: always validates current state Neutral, because potentially more/less events/requests: reduces communication overhead when related entity is on average used less than once, otherwise increases overhead Bad, because results in tight coupling between services: relation to foreign entity can only be created if synchronous request to other service is successful
documents.s: send a request to the owning service to verify that the entity with a given ID is valid

documents.t: APIs should support some kind of pagination when accessing a list of entities, to allow limiting API results to a managable size. In GraphQL, two approaches are popular: offset based pagination, where one specifies an offset in the list, and cursor based pagination, where a string serves as cursor to some element in the list.

cursor-based pagination (according to connection specification) with totalCount support cursor-based pagination (according to connection specification) without totalCount support offset-based pagination with totalCount support offset-based pagination without totalCount support
Chosen option: "offset-based pagination with totalCount support", because easier to implement, and we need totalCount support for the frontend.

A typical GraphQL schema will look like this: type Query { "Get a list of entities" entities( "Number of items to skip" skip: Int "Number of items to return" first: Int "Ordering" orderby: EntityOrder ): EntityConnection! } "A connection to a list of Entity values." type EntityConnection { "Whether this connection has a next page" hasNextPage: Boolean! "The resulting entities." nodes: [Entity!]! "The total amount of items in this connection" totalCount: Int! } "Entity order" input EntityOrderInput { "The field to order by" field: EntityOrderField "The direction to order by" direction: OrderDirection } "Order direction" enum OrderDirection { "Ascending order" ASC "Descending order" DESC } "Entity order fields" enum EntityOrderField { "Order entities by their id" ID } If the entity type of the connection is managed by a different service, knowledge of the entities is limited (typically only the id of the referenced entity is available). Thus, ordering support is limited to ID. To prevent conflicts between GraphQL type names, the following types MUST be used instead of EntityOrderInput: "Common order" input CommonOrderInput { "The field to order by" field: CommonOrderField "The direction to order by" direction: OrderDirection } "Common order fields" enum CommonOrderField { "Order entities by their id" ID }

Good, because hides away technical implementation (can be offset based or different) Good, because standardized: https://relay.dev/graphql/connections.htm Good, because includes totalCount support, allowing clients to display the amount of pages Bad, because difficult to implement: cursors have to encode values of all fields used in ordering Bad, because does not easily allow to start at an arbitrary offset
documents.s: cursor-based pagination (according to connection specification) with totalCount support

documents.t: Good, because hides away technical implementation (can be offset based or different) Good, because standardized: https://relay.dev/graphql/connections.htm Bad, because difficult to implement: cursors have to encode values of all fields used in ordering Bad, because does not easily allow to start at an arbitrary offset Bad, because clients cannot fetch the total amount of elements, which is necessary for some frontend designs
documents.s: cursor-based pagination (according to connection specification) without totalCount support

documents.t: Good, because allows to start at arbitrary offset Good, because easier to implement compared to cursor-based pagination (in particular with SQL databases) Good, because includes totalCount support, allowing clients to display the amount of pages Bad, because less flexible than cursor-based pagination Bad, because non-standard

Good, because allows to start at arbitrary offset Good, because easiest to implement (in particular with SQL databases), due to missing totalCount support Bad, because clients cannot fetch the total amount of elements, which is necessary for some frontend designs Bad, because less flexible than cursor-based pagination Bad, because non-standard

Category and product service are tightly coupled: The majority of endpoints of each services needs to either invoke the other service or duplicate the others services data: A product variant holds a characteristic value, this needs to be either saved in the product or category service. It is valuable to know if product variants with specific characteristic actually exist, when displaying the product categories in the frontend with each category characteristic. This prevents the user from running into empty queries. We only want to show characteristics to filter by, if product variants of the specific characteristic exists. Therefore the category service needs to know about the current product variants. Similarly, displaying a product variant includes its characteristic value, for example: size M for a t-shirt. Each product variant request depends on the characteristic value. A category can only be deleted if no product points to it. Should a category automatically be deleted if the last product referencing the category is deleted?

Save characteristic values of product variants in product service and let category service request the needed information Save characteristic values of product variants in category service and let product service request the needed information Data duplication in both services Merge category and product service Shared database (not up to discussion)

Chosen option: "Merge category and product service", because we have enough services to fulfill our requirement of 15 services. We do not see a clear cut that would still allow us to implement the functionality we want out of the system.
Good, querying products / product variants does not require querying the category service Bad, vice versa Bad, queries induce overhead
documents.s: Save characteristic values of product variants in product service and let category service request the needed information

documents.t: Good, filtering products / product variants for categories does not require querying the product service Bad, vice versa Bad, queries induce overhead
documents.s: Save characteristic values of product variants in category service and let product service request the needed information

documents.t: Bad, can lead to consistency problems Good, sync might create less overhead than just queries Bad, data duplications requires more memory
Bad, merging services reduces separation of concerns Good, prevents significant network overhead Neutral, one service less in total
A category hierarchy would enable better navigation of categories in the frontend.
Category hierarchy No Category hierarchy
No Category hierarchy, simpler and good enough.

Good, extensive filter options Neutral, hierarchy tree needs to be kept consistent Bad, removing a category gets more complicated Good, higher flexibility of assigning categories. Unsuitable super-categories can be avoided

Good, simpler as each category is independent of each other Good, hierarchy layer could be added dynamically if required: Bad, user might lose track of the category context: For example: A category bow might represent weapons or tools for the violin

The frontend needs to be able to send requests to the backend. As such requests need to be instrumented with tracing metadata, and authentication needs to be performed, an API gateway should be provided. However, different API technologies exist, and we need to decide which one to use
GraphQL REST gRPC Web
Chosen option: "GraphQL", because allows frontend to use one unified API to fetch data from the backend

Good, because allows frontend to easily fetch and combine data from different sources in the backend Neutral, because requires us to find a way how to combine API of different services into one unified GraphQL API

Good, because allows frontend to easily fetch and combine data from different sources in the backend Good, because built-in strict type system Good, because GraphiQL allows users to explore the API, including with a graphical interface to build requests Good, because allows client to define which data should be fetched, reducing overfetching Good, because allows to solve N+1 problem of requests, as instead of multiple consecutive requests, a single request can define all data which needs to be fetched Bad, because more difficult to implement, in particular regarding authorization

Good, because easiest to implement, in particular regarding authentication Good, because easy to use for users of the reference architecture Good, because good for browser caching Neutral, because no built-in typing, but could be provided via OpenAPI Bad, because more difficult to combine data from different sources: combine in gateway? have all routes from services in gateway? Bad, because does not allow frontend to define which data should be fetched

Good, because allows streaming Good, because built-in type system Good, because binary protocol is more efficient Neutral, because binary format is more difficult to understand in web environment, in particular form users of the reference architecture, but text-based protocol could also be used. JSON could also be used, but it is non-standard. Bad, because less common in web environments compared to other options Bad, because compared to GraphQL, does not provide unified way for client to define which data should be fetched
We consider two different ways of building our frontend: either a micro frontend, or a monolithic frontend.
micro frontend monolithic frontend

Chosen option: "monolithic frontend", because with the given resources (5 developers) and experiences, a monolithic frontend is easier to implement and thus allows us to focus on more important aspects of the reference architecture

Good, because we do not need to consider requirements of a micro frontend with respect to the API gateway Neutral, because there is a single repository for the frontend. Good, because the frontend tech stack can be chosen rather freely. We decided to use Vue with Vuetify to create a single page web application. Neutral, because all services have to support the API gateway for the frontend.

Good, because would result in our whole stack to be "micro" from end-to-end Good, because would frontends of services to be developed independently Bad, because more difficult to implement, in particular with regard to given resources Bad, because there are several frontend components, which are not clearly associated with a specific backend service

Good, because easier to implement, thus allowing us to focus on more important aspects of the reference architecture Good, because simplifies defining frontend components independant of backend service structure Bad, because results in whole stack not being "micro" end-to-end

In the following, an Entity of the domain model is formatted verbatim, and a Bounded Context is formatted italic.
documents.s: Design Choices in the Domain Model

documents.t: The domain model does not automatically add returned items back to the Inventory. They need to be added manually, similar to a shipment from the manufacturer, or thrown away.

A User cannot reserve items by putting them in the ShoppingCart: The Inventory contains ProductItems as instances of a ProductVariant. These ProductItems do not know about existing ShoppingCarts or ShoppingCartItems. Thus, when the user wants to finish the order, the system must check whether the ProductItem is still in stock, since it could have been ordered by another user in the meantime. A User can have exactly one ShoppingCart at a time, independent of the client device used.

An Invoice is associated with exactly one Order. Split invoices for parts of the order are not supported. A Payment is only created after the associated Invoice has been paid. The store supports only payment methods where the payment is completed before the shipping of any ordered items.

A Coupon is a promotional offer. It allows a User to apply a Discount to a Product. Each Coupon is associated with a specific Discount. The store only offers Discounts in percentages. The store does not have gift cards. The Discount can have whitelist filters by Product, ProductVariant, and Category. If filters are present, the Discount can be applied to Products matching any filter. If no filters are present, the Discount can be applied to all products.

Each ProductVariantVersion has its TaxRate specified. Thus, the store cannot easily operate in multiple tax rate regions. Tax deduction for business accounts are not supported by the system, since the TaxRate is fixed for all Users.

A Notification is within-the-system; they are not forwarded to emails, text messages, or anything else. Notifications are read-only. Users can only receive notifications, they cannot reply to them. Each Notification is sent to exactly one user. There are no broadcasts.

To be able to order something from the store, a User needs an Account. This implies that the store does not support something like "checkout as guest". Addresses stored by the system do not have to be unique physical addresses. The system may store multiple instances of the same physical address. One Address cannot be related to multiple User instances. A user's Wishlists are private and cannot be seen by other users.
documents.s: How Users relate to their Account, Wishlists, and Addresses

documents.t: The store has no Category hierarchy.
Every service exposes a health check endpoint under GET /health. When the service is healthy, a 200 OK status is returned. When the service is unhealthy, a 5XX status is returned.
Browsing the online shop​ Looking for a specific product​ Looking for a specific product (Alternative 1)​
Making an order​ Checking my order's status​ Returning an ordered item​
Writing a review​ Writing a review (Alternativ 1)​
Adding a new wishlist​ Adding an item to the wishlist​
Adding a new product that only has one variant​ Adding a new product that has multiple variants​
Software Artifacts Product Domain Usability Requirements Non-functional Requirements Configurations Design Architecture

R-SArt-001: Documentation​ The system, including its architecture, must be well documented. Design decisions must be documented using Architectural Design Records (ADRs). R-SArt-002: Clean code​ The code must be reviewed for quality, especially clarity and brevity. R-SArt-003: Clean and simple deployment​ A one-click deployment is not strictly required, but the deployment process must be as simple and clean as possible. The README file referring to the deployment must be complete, well-structured, informative, easy to read and to understand, and always kept up to date. A new administrator without prior knowledge about the system should be able to deploy the system within 2 hours by following the instructions in the README file. R-SArt-005: Chaos testing​ The system must be tested using chaos testing. Depending on the selected chaos testing toolkit, the system must support the dynamic injection of faults and network conditions (e.g., packet loss, latency) to simulate failure scenarios. Regarding the targeted non-functional requirement at test -- reliability --: Either the toolkit simulates unreliability or the system must support different configurations of reliability itself.

warning Domain​ R-D-001: The domain must be specific and self-explanatory​ The domain must be a specific one. Researchs must be able to understand the domain intuitively. The domain must not be an obstacle under any circumstances. As long as the domain is self-explanatory, it doesn't matter which domain is shown. The domain must not require special knowledge about it in order to be able to use the system. R-D-002: The domain should not be part of the technology world​ The domain should be clearly separated from any technology domain to avoid confusion regarding wordings and terms. There should be no clashes regarding the language of the domain and the language of the system's architecture: no common wordings. e.g., words like service, pipeline, container (Docker) should be avoided in the domain language

R-D-001: The domain must be specific and self-explanatory​ The domain must be a specific one. Researchs must be able to understand the domain intuitively. The domain must not be an obstacle under any circumstances. As long as the domain is self-explanatory, it doesn't matter which domain is shown. The domain must not require special knowledge about it in order to be able to use the system. R-D-002: The domain should not be part of the technology world​ The domain should be clearly separated from any technology domain to avoid confusion regarding wordings and terms. There should be no clashes regarding the language of the domain and the language of the system's architecture: no common wordings. e.g., words like service, pipeline, container (Docker) should be avoided in the domain language

R-U-003: Configurations​ The system exposes exactly one configuration file in a central location. This file must be independent of the number of microservices and their technology stacks. R-U-008: Observability​ The administrator must be able to observe and understand what the system is doing. To achieve this, fine-grained logging and monitoring must be in place. The current utilization (load) of the individual microservices must be reported via a OpenTelemetry monitoring API. R-U-014: Demo mode​ There should be a kind of demo mode so that a new user can try out the system quickly and easily: We need to provide test data with which the system can be directly loaded. R-U-018: Usage costs​ The system should measure and report the costs of running and scaling the system. As an administrator, I want to make decisions based on the costs of the system's usage. R-U-019: JMeter testing​ The system must be load tested using JMeter. The documentation should guide performance engineers in setting up test scenarios accurately within JMeter. The documentation should define clear API contracts or specifications (such as Swagger/OpenAPI documentation) for the System's endpoints, request formats, and responses. This helps in accurately configuring JMeter's HTTP Request samplers and assertions.

R-NFR-002: Elasticity / Auto scaling​ The system must be elastic. The system must be able to scale automatically. R-NFR-003: Resilience​ The system should be resilient. The systems' microservices must not fail suddenly without good reason. They should especially continue to operate when other microservices fail. R-NFR-004: Maintainability​ In order to improve maintainability, things should be kept as simple as possible. Instead of trying to support every possible deployment, only one deployment should be supported but that one should be supported very well.

R-Conf-004: Scaling policies​ The system should offer different alternatives for scaling. The system should allow the user to configure the scaling techniques in place. For each scaling strategy, the system should inform about the costs of the chosen scaling strategy. R-Conf-006: Configurations scope​ The system should allow for configurations on the following levels: System Microservice Database (Management System)

R-A-001: Extendability​ The System must be expandable: Additional microservices can be added without further ado. R-A-002: Scale out scenario for databases​ The system should account for a scale out scenario regarding databases. R-A-003: Distributed Transactions​ The system should support distributed transactions, ensuring the data integrity across multiple microservices and databases. R-A-005: Loose Coupling​ Microservices must be only loosely coupled. All communication must be via the other microservices' APIs. A domain-driven declaration of microservice responsibilities must be at hand. R-A-008: Distributed Tracing​ The system must have distributed tracing available.
The address service provides the bounded context Address. It is responsible for managing addresses, both for users and of the vendor itself.
info Imported entities from other bounded contexts are marked with a dashed border.
Language: Kotlin Framework: Spring Boot GraphQL Library: GraphQL Kotlin Database: PostgreSQL Schema Management: Flyway
This section lists events that are defined or used by the address service.

address/user-address/created​ This event is published when a new user address is created. { id: string, name?: Name, street1: string, street2: string, city: string, postalCode: string, country: string, companyName?: string, userId: string } address/vendor-address/created​ This event is published when a new vendor address is created. { id: string, name?: Name, street1: string, street2: string, city: string, postalCode: string, country: string, companyName?: string } address/user-address/archived​ This event is published when a user address is archived. { id: string, userId: string }
user/user/created: Used to validate that addresses belong to an existing users

The catalog service consists of the bounded contexts Catalog, Category, and CategoryValue. It is responsible for managing products, their variants and versions, categories, and their respective characteristics and values.
info Imported entities from other bounded contexts are marked with a dashed border.
Language: Kotlin Framework: Spring Boot GraphQL Library: GraphQL Kotlin Database: PostgreSQL Schema Management: Flyway
This section lists events that are defined or used by the catalog service.

catalog/product/created​ This event is published when a new product is created. { id: string, internalName: string, isPubliclyVisible: boolean, defaultVariantId: string, categoryIds: string[] } catalog/product-variant/created​ This event is published when a new product variant is created. This includes the default variant created when a product is created. { id: string, productId: string, currentVersionId: string, isPubliclyVisible: boolean } catalog/product-variant-version/created​ This event is published when a new product variant version is created. This includes the initial version created when a product variant is created. { id: string, name: string, description: string, version: number, retailPrice: number, createdAt: string, canBeReturnedForDays?: number, productVariantId: string, taxRateId: string, weight: number, mediaIds: string[] } catalog/category/created​ This event is published when a new category is created. { id: string, name: string, description: string } catalog/product/updated​ This event is published when a product is updated. { id: string, internalName: string, isPubliclyVisible: boolean, defaultVariantId: string, categoryIds: string[] } catalog/product-variant/updated​ This event is published when a product variant is updated. { id: string, isPubliclyVisible: boolean }
tax/tax-rate/created: Used to import TaxRate from the Tax bounded context. media/media/created: Used to import Media from the Media bounded context.
Merge Category and Product Service No category hierarchy
The discount service provides the bounded context Discount. It is responsible for managing discounts, coupons, and their usages by users.
info Imported entities from other bounded contexts are marked with a dashed border.
Language: Kotlin Framework: Spring Boot GraphQL Library: GraphQL Kotlin Database: PostgreSQL Schema Management: Flyway
This section lists events that are defined or used by the discount service.

discount/discount/created​ This event is published when a new discount is created. { id: string, discount: number, maxUsagesperUser?: number, validUntil: string, validFrom: string, minOrderAmount?: number, discountAppliesToCategoryIds: string[], discountAppliesToProductIds: string[], discountAppliesToProductVariantIds: string[] } discount/discount/updated​ This event is published when a discount is updated. { id: string, discount: number, maxUsagesperUser?: number, validUntil: string, validFrom: string, minOrderAmount?: number, discountAppliesToCategoryIds: string[], discountAppliesToProductIds: string[], discountAppliesToProductVariantIds: string[] } discount/coupon/created​ This event is published when a new coupon is created. { id: string, code: string, discountId: string, maxUsages: number, validUntil: string, validFrom: string } discount/coupon/updated​ This event is published when a coupon is updated. { id: string, code: string, discountId: string, maxUsages: number, validUntil: string, validFrom: string } discount/order/validation-succeeded​ This event is thrown when the validation of an order caused by inventory/product-item/reservation-succeeded (TODO link) is successful. { order: Order; } discount/order/validation-failed​ This event is thrown when the validation of an order caused by inventory/product-item/reservation-succeeded (TODO link) is not successful. { order: Order, failingDiscountIds: string[] }

inventory/product-item/reservation-succeeded: Causes a validation of the order to check if the discounts can still be applied. user/user/created: Used to import User from the User bounded context. catalog/category/created: Used to import Category from the Category bounded context. catalog/product/created: Used to import Product from the Catalog bounded context. catalog/product-variant/created: Used to import ProductVariant from the Catalog bounded context.

JavaScript Framework: Vue Programming Languages: mainly TypeScript and standard HTML within Vue components UI Library: Vuetify Package Manager: Yarn -- Instructions for the most important yarn commands can be found in the README.md in the repository. Tooling: ESLint, Prettier, GraphQL Codegen
Besides the various views to interact with the system, there are two important components in the frontend: The Pinia store and the GraphQL client.

The role of the Pinia Store in Vue is to manage the state of the application in a centralized and reactive manner, facilitating state manipulation and sharing across components. The store manages: login state the active user role the user's shopping cart the notification queue

The GraphQL client is the interface to the backend services. All communication with the backend is done through the GraphQL client. It provides functions for sending queries and mutations to the backend and receiving responses.

The gateway is the entry point for the MisArch system. It is responsible for routing requests to the correct service and handling authentication. Requests are forwarded via dapr service invocation to the respective service.
Language: TypeScript Framework: GraphQL Mesh
Use GraphQL API Gateway

The inventory service consists of the context ProductItem. It is responsible for managing the inventory - consisting of ProductItems of a ProductVariant. It is part of the order and return saga, where it reserves and releases the required ProductItems.
info Imported entities from other bounded contexts are marked with a dashed border.
Language: TypeScript Framework: NestJS GraphQL Library: @nestjs/graphql with @nestjs/apollo (code first) Database: MongoDB Entity Management: Mongoose
This section lists events that are defined or used by the inventory service.

inventory/product/created​ This event is published when a new product is created. { id: string, internalName: string, isPubliclyVisible: boolean, defaultVariantId: string, categoryIds: string[] } inventory/product-item/reservation-succeeded​ This event is published when all ProductItems for the ProductVariants of an Orders OrderItems were reserved successfully. { order: Order; } inventory/product-item/reservation-failed​ This event is published when at least one ProductItem for the ProductVariants of an Orders OrderItems could not be reserved. { order: Order, failedProductVariantIds: string[] }

catalog/product-variant/created: Used to import ProductVariant from the Catalog bounded context. order/order/created: Used to reserve the ProductItems for an Order. payment/payment/payment-enabled: Send Inventory to Fullfillment payment/payment/payment-failed: Releases Product Items related to the order shipment/shipment/status-updated: Updates the inventory status based on the shipment status shipment/shipment/created: Updates the inventory status based on the shipment status discount/order/validation-failed: Releases Product Items related to the order
Check Entity Consistency
The invoice service provides the bounded context Invoice. It is responsible for managing invoices of orders.
info Imported entities from other bounded contexts are marked with a dashed border.
The service provides a GraphQL API, which is documented in the GraphQL API documentation (TODO).
Language: Rust Framework: axum GraphQL Library: async_graphql Database: MongoDB
This section lists events that are defined or used by the invoice service.
invoice/invoice/created​ This event is published when an invoice for an order is created. { order: Order, invoice: Invoice, }

discount/order/validation-succeeded: Triggers this service to create an invoice and emit following event: invoice/invoice/created address/vendor-address/created: Used to obtain vendor address. user/user/created: Used to synchronize user addresses on users. address/user-address/created: Used to obtain user address. address/user-address/archived: Used to obtain user address.

The media service provides the bounded context Media. It is responsible for managing media files such as images. The media services uses a Nginx reverse proxy to make the fixed domain of MinIO pre-signed URLs configurable through environment variables. A pre-signed URL is used as a reference to a specific media. It is generated for each Media entity when the path attribute is requested. This is necessary for access control to specific media files. A Media entity cannot be queried by a user client directly. It is used in within the service mesh by other services to reference medias in the services datatypes. Pre-signed URLs link media to authenticated service datatypes, chaining authentication. Each request of the path attribute on Media generates a new pre-signed URL with a limited validity duration. In other words, presigned URLS are generated automatically without any interference and attachments will always vanish once the presigned URL is no longer valid The service parses the following environment variables: MINIO_ENDPOINT: configurable domain of the MinIO instance. PATH_EXPIRATION_TIME: validity duration of MinIO pre-signed URLs in seconds. A resource can no longer be reached under the pre-signed URL after the pre-signed URL is expired. PROXY_PATH: path to include in the pre-signed URLs.
Language: Rust Framework: axum GraphQL Library: async_graphql Database: MinIO Reverse Proxy: Nginx nginx
This section lists events that are defined or used by the media service.
media/media/created​ This event is published when a media is created. { id: string; }
The notification service provides the bounded context Notification. It is responsible for managing notifications.
info Imported entities from other bounded contexts are marked with a dashed border.
Language: Kotlin Framework: Spring Boot GraphQL Library: GraphQL Kotlin Database: PostgreSQL Schema Management: Flyway
This section lists events that are defined or used by the notification service.

notification/notification/create: Other services can publish this event to create new notifications. { title: string, body: string, userId: boolean } user/user/created: Used to validate that only existing users can receive notifications
The order service provides the bounded context Order. It is responsible for managing orders of users.
info Imported entities from other bounded contexts are marked with a dashed border.
Language: Rust Framework: axum GraphQL Library: async_graphql Database: MongoDB
This section lists events that are defined or used by the order service.

order/order/created​ This event is published when an order is placed, which means its order status is set to PLACED. { id: string; userId: string; createdAt: string; orderStatus: OrderStatus; placedAt: string; rejectionReason?: RejectionReason; orderItems: OrderItem[]; shipmentAddressId: string; invoiceAddressId: string; compensatableOrderAmount: number; paymentInformationId: string; paymentAuthorization?: PaymentAuthorization; vatNumber?: string; } order/order-compensation/created​ This event is published when an order should be compensated due to a failed shipment. { id: string, orderId: string, orderItemIds: string[], triggeredAt: DateTime, amountToCompensate: number }

catalog/product-variant-version/created: Used to obtain the current product variant versions for product variants. discount/coupon/created: Used to validate the existence of coupons. tax/tax-rate-version/created: Used to obtain the current tax rate versions for tax rates. shipment/shipment-method/created: Used to validate the existence of shipment methods. user/user/created: Used to validate that orders belong to an existing user. address/user-address/created: Used to validate that addresses exist for a user. address/user-address/archived: Used to validate that addresses exist for a user. shipment/shipment/creation-failed: Used to trigger order compensation for a shipment.

The payment service consists of the bounded contexts Payment, PaymentInformation and PaymentMethod. It is responsible for handling the payment process and managing Users payment information. It is part of the order and return saga, where it captures the payments for Orders or refunds the payment after an Return. It is not a payment provider but simulates communicating with one via the Simulation Service.
The service provides a REST API which is used by the external payment provider to inform about payment status changes.
Language: TypeScript Framework: NestJS GraphQL Library: @nestjs/graphql with @nestjs/apollo (code first) Database: MongoDB Entity Management: Mongoose
This section lists events that are defined or used by the payment service.

payment/payment/payment-enabled​ This event is published when all steps necessary to be able to process a payment were successfully completed. It indicates, that other services can continue the order saga. { order: Order; } payment/payment/payment-processed​ This event is published when the full payment amount was successfully captured. { order: Order; } payment/payment/payment-failed​ This event is published when the payment fails finally after its retries. { order: Order, }

user/user/created: Used to create default payment Methods for each user when created. discount/order/validation-succeeded: Used to start the payment process after the previous steps finished successfully.
The return service provides the bounded context Return. It is responsible for managing and validating returns.
info Imported entities from other bounded contexts are marked with a dashed border.
Language: Kotlin Framework: Spring Boot GraphQL Library: GraphQL Kotlin Database: PostgreSQL Schema Management: Flyway
This section lists events that are defined or used by the return service.
return/return/created​ This event is published when a new user return is created. { id: string, orderId: string, orderItemsIds: string[], reason: string, refundedAmount: number, createdAt: string }

shipment/shipment/created: Used to import Shipment from the Shipment bounded context. shipment/shipment/status-updated: Used to update the delivery status of imported Shipment entities. order/order/created: Used to import Order and associated OrderItems from the Order bounded context. catalog/product-variant-version/created: Used to import ProductVariantVersion from the Product bounded context.

user/user/created: Used to validate that reviews belong to an existing user catalog/product-variant/created: Used to validate that product variants contained in reviews exist
The shipment service provides the bounded context Shipment. It is responsible for managing shipments and their state, and interacts with an external shipment provider to do so.
info Imported entities from other bounded contexts are marked with a dashed border.
The service provides a REST API which is used by the external shipment provider to inform about shipment status changes.
Updates the status of a shipment. Body { status: ShipmentStatus; } enum ShipmentStatus { PENDING, IN_PROGRESS, DELIVERED, FAILED }
Language: Kotlin Framework: Spring Boot GraphQL Library: GraphQL Kotlin Database: PostgreSQL Schema Management: Flyway
This section lists events that are defined or used by the shipment service.

shipment/shipment/created​ This event is published when a new shipment is created. { id: string, orderId?: string, returnId?: string, status: ShipmentStatus, orderItemIds: string[], shipmentMethodId: string, shipmentAddressId: string } shipment/shipment/creation-failed​ This event is published when the creation of a shipment failed. { orderId?: string, returnId?: string, orderItemIds: string[], shipmentMethodId: string, shipmentAddressId: string, reason: string } shipment/shipment/status-updated​ This event is published when the status of a shipment is updated. { id: string, orderId?: string, returnId?: string, status: ShipmentStatus, orderItemIds: string[], } shipment/shipment-method/created​ This event is published when a new shipment method is created. { id: string, name: string, description: string, ref: string, baseFees: number, feesPerItem: number, feesPerKg: number } shipment/shipment-method/archived​ This event is published when a shipment method is archived. { id: string; }

payment/payment/payment-enabled: Used to create new shipments for an order when the order and associated payment is validated successfully. address/user-address/created: Used to import UserAddress from the Address bounded context. address/vendor-address/created: Used to import VendorAddress from the Address bounded context. catalog/product-variant-version/created: Used to import ProductVariantVersion from the Catalog bounded context. return/return/created: Used to create a shipment for a return.

The tax service provides the bounded context Tax. It is responsible for managing tax rates and their versions.

tax/tax-rate/created​ This event is published when a new tax rate is created. { id: string, name: string, description: string, currentVersionId: string } tax/tax-rate-version/created​ This event is published when a new tax rate version is created. This includes the initial version created when a tax rate is created. { id: string, rate: number, version: number, createdAt: string, taxRateId: string }

user/user/create​ Published by Keycloak to inform the user service about a newly created user account. { id: string, username: string, firstName: string, lastName: string }
